ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E (Flat bread) used as the closest defensible category match for Tissue Prata, a thin crispy flatbread. No exact FoodEx2 entry exists for this specific Singaporean Indian preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Tissue Prata or equivalent flatbread preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Tissue Prata is approximately 80 g, equivalent to one full piece as served at a Singapore hawker stall or mamak restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Tissue Prata — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tissue Prata. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical Singapore primary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on plain roti canai / paratha (wheat flour flatbread, pan-fried with fat)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tissue Prata specifically. GI of 62 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain roti canai / paratha (wheat flour flatbread, pan-fried with ghee), which has a reported GI of approximately 62 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 75.23 × 0.80) / 100 = 35.2 (serving size 80 g, carbohydrate per serving ≈ 60.2 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 632 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Tissue Prata to a small portion (≤ 40 g) and avoid pairing with high-sodium curry sauces or dhal, as the combined sodium load can significantly elevate blood pressure; requesting preparation with reduced salt and minimal ghee is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load (75.2 g/100 g) with medium GI (~62) from refined wheat flour base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict Tissue Prata to a very small portion (≤ 40 g) and pair it with a protein-rich or low-GI accompaniment to blunt the post-meal glucose spike, as the high refined carbohydrate content will substantially raise blood glucose despite the moderating effect of 10.2 g/100 g dietary fibre.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (632 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from ghee; cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Tissue Prata poses concern primarily due to its high sodium content (632 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from ghee used in preparation; portion size should be limited to ≤ 40 g and preparation with less ghee is recommended to reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 632 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold; refined carbohydrate base promotes systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Tissue Prata as the high sodium content (632 mg/100 g) may worsen joint swelling and the refined wheat flour base contributes to a high glycaemic load that drives systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tissue Prata (also known as Roti Tisu) is a distinctively thin, crispy variant of the classic Singaporean Indian flatbread prata. Unlike the thicker, softer plain prata, Tissue Prata is stretched to an extremely thin sheet and pan-fried with ghee on a flat griddle until it becomes paper-thin and golden-brown with a shatteringly crisp texture. It is typically shaped into a tall cone or folded into a fan shape for presentation. The dough is made from wheat flour, water, ghee, egg, and salt, laminated through repeated folding and stretching. Per 100 g, it provides 412 kcal, 9.85 g protein, 8.25 g fat, and 75.23 g carbohydrate with 10.2 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 632 mg/100 g. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sodium and refined carbohydrate content. It is a popular hawker and mamak restaurant item in Singapore, commonly served with sugar, condensed milk, or curry dipping sauce. It is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and is a staple at Indian Muslim food stalls.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the canonical primary source for Singapore dishes. The food is an exact match in the HPB database. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on plain roti canai / paratha, which is the closest approved-source equivalent.

GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on plain roti canai / paratha from the University of Sydney GI Database — no direct GI measurement for Tissue Prata was found in approved sources.
